title: Connecting the dots without a brain
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Making rice regrow year after year, our shared love of song, and more
Brought to you by Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
View this email in your browser
20 March 2026
Today’s Future News explores the unexpected memory of a single-celled organism. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including our shared love of beautiful sounds.
Agricultural Genetics  |  News from Science
Genetic tinkering could make rice more like apples
Perennial-like rice that grows horizontal branches (red) can sprout new plants that live another year.  Bingxin Dai and Danfeng Lv
Imagine a grain that comes up every year, with no need to till fields and replant each crop. Farmers would save labor; soil erosion would slow. In 2018, researchers in China introduced a rice variety that can be harvested several years in row, but creating this strain took decades of traditional breeding.

Finding the right genes from a wild species could speed up this process for new varieties of perennial rice tailored for different regions and growing conditions. This week in Science, a team identified two such genes that help the wild rice Oryza rufipogon flourish year after year. They added these genes to the cultivated rice O. sativa , which gave it a key perennial trait: the ability to stop flowering and resume vegetative growth, instead of dying off after flowering as annual plants generally do.

In the new study, the team first crossed a commonly studied strain of O. rufipogon with a research variety of cultivated rice to create many offspring, each with a different small segment of DNA from the wild species. Looking over the grown plants, they selected one that had the essential ability to stop flowering and resume vegetative growth, plants easily identified from their prolific branching stems called tillers. The researchers discovered MIR156B and MIR156C as the genes responsible for the late growth. Unfortunately, these plants produced sterile flowers that yielded no grain.

Other genes are clearly needed, the team acknowledged. They are now exploring genes that might restore fertility to the secondary tillers and investigating genes expressed in underground tissues such as roots and rhizomes, to see whether they might support perennial growth and other desirable traits for cultivated crops.
read the Science Paper
Read the full story
Comparative Biology  |  Science
Music to our ears
Charles Darwin once declared that birds, which he described as “the most aesthetic of all animals” besides humans, have “nearly the same taste for the beautiful as we have.” Indeed, many of the signals animals use to attract mates—from the peacock’s dazzling tail to the humpback whale’s song—also delight humans. Now, a new study suggests our acoustic preferences overlap with a range of other species , lending additional support to Darwin’s hypothesis.

Researchers gathered 110 pairs of sounds produced by 16 nonhuman animal species—spanning insects, frogs, birds, and mammals—and played them for more than 4000 human study participants, who were asked to rate which of the two sounds they liked more. In general, human preferences for animal sounds lined up with what the animals themselves found attractive, and the agreement was higher when the animals had stronger preferences. While the team didn’t find any significant difference between humans with experience identifying animal sounds, such as birders, and nonexperts, people who spent more time listening to music tended to agree more with animals. Humans and animals alike also preferred sounds with acoustic adornments, such as trills and clicks.

“Taken together, these results confirm that a single species can harbor similar preferences to a taxonomically broad range of species,” the study authors explained. The findings also serve as a reminder, they added, “that much of the beauty we find in nature was intended for receivers other than ourselves.”
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology: Call for Entries 2026
This prize is awarded to young scientists for their outstanding contributions to neurobiological research based on experimental methods of molecular, cellular, systems, or organismic biology. Researchers not older than 35 years are invited to apply.
Apply Now
Future News
When feeding, single-celled Stentor looks a bit like a trumpet.  Flupke59 via WIKIMEDIA COMMONS | CC BY-SA
Tap, tap, tap: Stentor is listening
Today, kids often learn that you can estimate the distance of a storm by counting the seconds between seeing a lightning flash and hearing thunder: a mile for every five seconds (or a kilometer for every three). The connection between the flash and the bang just seems so obvious. But for millennia—from the time of Aristotle right up until the 1750s —even some of the smartest people on the planet thought that thunder preceded lightning, which just goes to show how tricky associative learning really is.

Learning the connection between a stimulus and an outcome is a complex cognitive feat, one that was long thought to require a brain and therefore arose in animals with complex nervous systems. Then box jellies demonstrated the ability, suggesting animals with any kind of neuronal connections can do it. But now, in a bioRxiv preprint, a team of researchers has found an even more unlikely organism connecting stimuli: the single-celled pond-dweller Stentor coeruleus.

Stentor has long fascinated microbiologists with their apparent knack for learning. Each large trumpet-shaped cell anchors itself underwater so it can filter out tiny scraps to eat. Because they are stuck in place, these protozoans have few options for avoiding danger. But when they do sense a potentially harmful disturbance, they do what they can—mostly, contract into a small ball. When researchers gently harass these single-celled organisms, they become less likely to react to a mechanical stimulus over time, a kind of learning known as habituation . But controversial studies suggesting associative learning in other unicellular critters got a team of researchers wondering: Can Stentor connect two stimuli?

To find out, they first demonstrated habituation by strongly tapping on a Stentor’s container every 45 seconds. As expected, the tiny trumpets almost always contracted for the first few taps, then became less likely to do so over time. They also tested whether weaker taps elicited the same kind of habituation; sure enough, though Stentors were less likely to contract in the first place, they became even less likely to do so after repeated taps. Next, instead of simply tapping once, the team tried tapping twice—a gentle tap one second before a forceful one. This time, the cells became more responsive to weak taps at first, then less responsive to all taps over time. Additional experiments ruled out several other explanations for such a pattern, leaving the researchers with the conclusion that Stentor first learns the association between the weak and strong taps, then habituates to both.

“It’s fascinating that a single cell can do such complex things that we thought required a brain, that required neurons, that required behavioural learning,” organismal biophysicist Shashank Shekhar told New Scientist. And Stentor  may not be unique among unicellular organisms. “My gut feeling is if it’s there once, it’s going to be there more,” he said.

“Our findings have broad implications for the evolutionary origins of associative learning,” the team wrote. After all, if a single-celled organism like Stentor can do it, “then there must be a cellular mechanism for such learning that does not depend on synaptic modification.” What that mechanism is, exactly, remains unknown. But what’s especially intriguing about the idea is that such learning could also occur in our much more complex minds. “It has been suggested that such mechanisms might also operate in the brain—a conjecture that invites a new program of research bridging the study of associative learning in protozoa and metazoa,” they concluded.
Read the preprint
podcast
Rethinking the peopling of the Americas, and the best ways to get groundwater back
By Sarah Crespi, Lizzie Wade   |   19 March 2026
Et Cetera
Build it and it’ll clean water
Artificial wetlands on floating platforms can pull pollutants from wastewater and break them down, much in the same way natural wetlands do, a study has found. And perhaps just as importantly, they clean the water up without emitting tons of greenhouse gases—a major downside to the alternative, microbial remediation. “In some ways it’s really complicated, but actually in other ways it’s really simple,” explained one microbiologist not involved with the study. “You don’t need a huge, very expensive facility to deal with this. You just need to grow some plants.”
EarthArxiv preprint  |  Read more at News from Science
A cream for frostbite?
Everyone knows that when it’s super cold outside, you need to bundle up. People who live, work, and explore especially frigid environments run the risk of frostbite even when they do wear warm gear. But in a yet-to-be-published study, researchers genetically engineered skin-living bacteria to generate twice the heat they usually do and to contain temperature-sensitive genes. They also showed these bacteria can survive when mixed into a cream, though whether the cream actually keeps skin warmer remains to be tested. “It’s very creative work. You can imagine this cream being the difference between getting frostbite or not,” said one expert who wasn’t involved in the research.
Read more at New Scientist
Math’s Nobel awarded for number theory
The 2026 Abel Prize, one of the most prestigious awards in mathematics and sometimes called math’s Nobel, was awarded to Gerd Faltings for his work proving that certain arithmetic equations have a finite number of solutions. “He’s a towering figure in number theory,” said the chairman of the prize committee. According to the prize citation, “his ideas and results have reshaped the field, settling major longstanding conjectures while also establishing new frameworks that have guided decades of subsequent work.”
Abel Prize Announcement  |  Read more at The New York Times
Last but not least
March Madness is in full swing here in the United States, perhaps the most important bracket until Fat Bear Week. Of course, I’m not talking about basketball; I’m talking about March Mammal Madness! Who do you have winning it all?
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Erik Stokstad and Phie Jacobs
